titus_atticus_convo_1_n01.npcDialogue: Sit. Don't touch the wine — it's Falernian, and I'm rationing it because nothing reliable comes up from the south anymore. War has a way of sorting men into two heaps: the ones it enriches and the ones it buries. I am, at present, in the former heap, but even I can feel the ground shifting. The whole financial landscape is remaking itself — fortunes sprouting overnight in army supply contracts, fortunes rotting in eastern trade exposure. I thought you deserved to hear the arithmetic from someone who learned to count without his sandals off.

responses.playerDialogue: You didn't summon me here for a tutorial. Where's the opportunity?
War rewards the swift. Show me where the money is moving and I'll place myself in its path.
Men are bleeding out on Italian hillsides while financiers tally their logistics margins. That doesn't sit well.
The Italian towns caught between the armies — someone must be extending them credit, finding them grain. Who?

fallbackResponse.playerDialogue: I'm listening. Tell me what you see from behind the ledger.

titus_atticus_convo_1_n02.npcDialogue: Three doors, since you want specifics. First — army supply contracts. The legions devour grain, leather, iron, and the publicani who normally furnish it are bankrupt or scattered. A man with capital and the right introductions steps through that gap, he's wealthy inside six months. Second — land in Campania. Prices have collapsed; the revolt has turned sellers into beggars. Buy now, hold through the peace, triple your outlay. Wars end, Crispus — they always end. Third — the eastern contracts. Mithridates hasn't struck, but the shrewd money is already retreating. The gamble is whether you retreat with it and swallow the known loss, or hold your position and wager that Rome stabilizes Asia before he moves. I've already chosen my door. Now you choose yours.

options.playerDialogue: Put my name on the supply contracts. I want a position in the wartime economy.
I'd rather carry this intelligence to my faction. Financial knowledge is a sharper weapon than most.
The eastern contracts. Before you finalize your exit — let me negotiate a cut of the position.
Interesting topography. But I'm not ready to plant a flag — let me think.

titus_atticus_convo_1_n03.npcDialogue: Trouble me? Everything troubles me. The debased coinage troubles me. The silence from my agents in Delos troubles me. This draft in my study — which I've spent good money failing to repair — troubles me enormously. But I don't confuse discomfort with paralysis. The war proceeds whether I profit or not — the only question is whether the men managing the money are competent enough to keep the whole apparatus from seizing up. I'd rather it were me than some senator's third son with a wine habit and a head for nothing. Now — the practical matter. The war is remaking the landscape. Do you want to understand how, or would you prefer to keep your moral garments spotless?

options.playerDialogue: Tell me. I'd rather be useful and compromised than righteous and blind.
I'll hear you out. But I'm not promising to play by your rules — I have my own compass.

titus_atticus_convo_1_n04.npcDialogue: Good. A decision — that alone puts you ahead of most people who've warmed that chair. I'll have the terms drawn up. One warning, offered freely: financial exposure constrains your political movement. Once you're profiting from the war economy, you can't back any faction that threatens to upset it. That's not a condition I impose — it's just arithmetic asserting itself. Your interests and mine are now partially aligned. And partial alignment is the only variety I find trustworthy.

options.playerDialogue: I understand the geometry. Send me the terms.

titus_atticus_convo_1_n05.npcDialogue: Ah. So you came not as an investor but as a courier. I'd be wounded if I weren't quietly impressed by the efficiency. Which faction receives the benefit of my analysis? Choose deliberately — once I know where my numbers are traveling, our conversation acquires a different character. I don't punish men for having loyalties, but I do recalibrate my candor.

options.playerDialogue: The optimates. The senatorial establishment needs to grasp the financial dimension of this war.
The populares. The people's advocates should know exactly who is gorging on their misery.
The Italian allies. They deserve to understand how Rome's treasury constrains her legions.
Sulla's circle. A commander who understands supply economics fights a different war entirely.

titus_atticus_convo_1_n06.npcDialogue: Remarkable. You're the first person who's tried to negotiate rather than simply accept or refuse. Most people treat a banker like a temple — walk in, take what's offered, leave a small prayer. You're treating me like a counterparty. That's either very clever or very foolish, and I suspect it's delightfully both. Fine — five percent of the eastern exit. It's not generous, but it's real coin, and in this economy real coin is scarcer than honest men in the senate.

options.playerDialogue: Five percent of your eastern position. Done.

titus_atticus_convo_1_n07.npcDialogue: We're in business, then. And I mean precisely that — business. Not friendship, not alliance, not any of the other sentimental drapery Romans throw over their financial arrangements. Though I'll confess, among my various associates, you are among the least tedious. That is the highest compliment in my vocabulary. My agent will find you when the first returns arrive. Do try not to get yourself killed in the interval — the dead are notoriously poor at collecting dividends.

titus_atticus_convo_1_n08.npcDialogue: The optimates. Old silver, old names, the ancestral establishment. They'll weaponize this efficiently — they always do when money's involved. Just understand that my next conversation with you will be considerably less forthcoming. You've turned my analysis into someone else's ammunition, and ammunition has a way of traveling in unwelcome directions. Go. I'll adjust my calculations to account for the leak.

titus_atticus_convo_1_n09.npcDialogue: The populares. A man of the people armed with actual data — that's either a reformer or a demagogue, and the distinction usually comes down to the quality of the man holding the numbers. I suppose time will sort it. My door stays open, but the Falernian stays corked. Go feed your faction, Crispus. I'll adjust my portfolio to absorb the disclosure.

titus_atticus_convo_1_n10.npcDialogue: No commitment. No exposure. The safest possible outcome — which is another way of saying the least interesting. You've acquired knowledge today without risking anything for it. That's a kind of profit, I suppose, though not the kind that compounds. The door remains open. I'm an optimist about people, which is peculiar given that I'm a pessimist about absolutely everything else.

titus_atticus_convo_1_n11.npcDialogue: The Italians. Now that's an unusual destination for my arithmetic — most Romans don't think of the allies as a faction worth arming with intelligence. Which means you're either sentimental about their cause or remarkably shrewd about their future. Either way, you've told me something important about yourself that I hadn't calculated for. My candor with you is now — adjusted. Go help your allies. I'll make my own arrangements.

titus_atticus_convo_1_n12.npcDialogue: Sulla's people. So you're making me a military man's banker — a role I hadn't auditioned for. Half my colleagues are already terrified of Sulla; now he'll have my financial modeling as well. Efficient of you. Dangerous for me. But I suppose managing that danger is your burden, not mine. Don't return expecting the same openness — a burnt source learns discretion very quickly.

deltas.reason: Atticus deepens the financial partnership — your commercial instincts have earned his trust

operations.reason: Delivered valuable financial intelligence to the optimate leadership

deltas.reason: Atticus considers you a political operative, not a serious financial mind

operations.reason: Armed the populares with financial intelligence about the war economy

deltas.reason: Atticus considers you a political operative, not a serious financial mind

operations.reason: Shared Roman financial vulnerabilities with the Italian confederation

deltas.reason: Atticus considers you a political operative, not a serious financial mind

operations.reason: Provided Sulla's circle with critical supply economics intelligence
